message:
fix(mcp/wa.events_annotate): derive changed-bit from caller intent (ft-xo3u4)

0a865e6f derived `changed` by READ-WRITE-READ-DIFF across four independent
storage calls with no surrounding transaction:

    prior = storage.get_event_annotations(...)
    storage.set_event_note(..., params.note, ...)
    ... audit ...
    current = storage.get_event_annotations(...)
    changed = prior.note != current.note      // ← TOCTOU

Two concurrent wa.events_annotate calls on the same event_id race:

    Client A TXN1: prior = None
    Client B TXN1: prior = None          (same pre-state)
    Client A TXN2: writes "foo"
    Client B TXN2: writes "bar"          (B overwrites A)
    Client A TXN4: reads "bar"           (A sees B's write)
    Client A => changed = (None != "bar") = true
                annotations.note = "bar"

Client A's envelope reports `changed: true, annotations.note: "bar"` —
but A wrote "foo". For a multi-agent swarm using this tool to leave
handoff notes on events, that is silent data loss dressed up as success.

Fix: derive `changed` from `prior_note != params.note` (this call's
requested note). `changed` now honestly reflects the caller's own intent
relative to the prior value observed by this call. Under concurrent
writes, Client A now reports `changed: true, annotations.note: "bar"` —
and a client that notices `annotations.note != their params.note` can
explicitly detect the overwrite. The on-disk annotations re-read is kept
so the envelope reflects the authoritative current state.

A fuller fix (option B from the review bead) would make set_event_note
return the prior note atomically from within the write transaction —
that's a larger ripple across WriteCommand + the sync helper signature
and is left for a follow-up. Option C, documented here, is the minimal
diff that stops misattributing another writer's value to this call.

Existing tests cover the serial {None→Some, Some→Some, None→None} cases
at mcp_tools.rs:5336-5398; all three still hold with the new derivation.

Agent: RusticMeadow

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

files.patch:
@@ -127,8 +127,8 @@
 {"id":"ft-b4mif","title":"[review][MEDIUM] README Quickstart claims ft --version emits rustc/target/features but it only emits semver+git","description":"README Quickstart section (landed in bcc668a0, Apr 23 13:54) claims:

  ft --version                 # semver + git commit + rustc + target + features

The clap version wiring at crates/frankenterm/src/main.rs:67-72 binds version = CLAP_VERSION.as_str() where CLAP_VERSION = build_meta::short_version() (lines 39-41). short_version() returns only:

  format!("{} ({}{})", frankenterm_core::VERSION, GIT_HASH, GIT_DIRTY)

i.e. semver + git commit + optional dirty marker. The rustc/target/features block lives in build_meta::verbose_version() (main.rs:43-61) and is ONLY reachable via the separate ft version --full subcommand (main.rs:16718-16724), NOT ft --version.

Without #[command(long_version = ...)] in the clap derive, ft --version and ft -V both surface short_version() only. A first-touch operator following the Quickstart will copy the command, not see what the docs promised, and have to chase where rustc/target/features actually show up.

Fix options:
1. Update README quickstart to:
     ft --version                 # semver + git commit (+ -dirty marker)
     ft version --full            # semver + git commit + built + rustc + target + features
2. OR add long_version = build_meta::verbose_version() to the Cli derive at crates/frankenterm/src/main.rs:72 so ft --version actually emits the full block (ft -V stays short).

Option 2 is a stronger fix because it makes the README literally true, but the operator expectation today is 'short for --version, full for version --full', and changing that flips the UX for every script that pipes ft --version into grep. Option 1 (doc fix) is safer.

Severity: MEDIUM — no functional bug, but first-touch operator expectation drift. Discovered in reviewer pane audit round 2, 2026-04-23.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-23T18:01:52.738804Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-23T18:04:33.492364Z","closed_at":"2026-04-23T18:04:33.492043Z","close_reason":"Doc fix path (b): README Quickstart step 2 now shows 'ft --version' for semver+short-git+dirty and 'ft version --full' for built/rustc/target/features, matching what crates/frankenterm/src/main.rs actually emits (short_version at :39-41, verbose_version at :43-61, version --full handler at :16718-16724). Binary behavior untouched so scripts grepping ft --version keep the short form. Landed in 86e08660.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["review"]}
 {"id":"ft-b5rbd","title":"[HIGH] GUI resize increment saturation can overflow backend min-size arithmetic","description":"Commit 193a9ebb changes `ResizeIncrementCalculator` to saturate `base_width`/`base_height` at the u16 ceiling (`crates/frankenterm-gui/src/resize_increment_calculator.rs:15-35`). Multiple window backends then immediately compute `base + increment` in u16 without guarding overflow (`frankenterm/window/src/os/macos/window.rs:1410-1412`, `frankenterm/window/src/os/x11/window.rs:1892-1893`, `frankenterm/window/src/os/wayland/window.rs:915-916`). With large padding/border/tab-bar geometry, the new saturation can produce `base_width == u16::MAX`, so `base_width + x` overflows before min-size clamping. This turns an intended overflow hardening change into a runtime panic/wraparound path on oversized layouts. Expected fix: clamp stored base dimensions to `u16::MAX - increment` (or widen backend arithmetic) and add a regression test that asserts `base + increment` stays representable.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-20T05:58:04.554054Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-20T06:42:08.945504Z","closed_at":"2026-04-20T06:42:08.945232Z","close_reason":"dup of ft-htf0i","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"ft-b677n","title":"[MEDIUM] wgpu readback poll loop has no test coverage for timeout, disconnect, or error-propagation paths","description":"Commit 6f41130c (br-43bbl fix wgpu readback polling) rewrites the
readback wait in crates/frankenterm-gui/src/termwindow/webgpu.rs:168-193
from:

\`\`\`rust
self.device.poll(PollType::Wait { submission_index: None, timeout: Some(WEBGPU_READBACK_TIMEOUT) })?;
receiver.recv_timeout(WEBGPU_READBACK_TIMEOUT)?...
\`\`\`

to a bounded poll loop:

\`\`\`rust
let deadline = Instant::now() + WEBGPU_READBACK_TIMEOUT;
loop {
    self.device.poll(wgpu::PollType::Poll)?;
    match receiver.recv_timeout(Duration::from_millis(10)) {
        Ok(result) => { result?; break; }
        Err(RecvTimeoutError::Timeout) if Instant::now() < deadline => continue,
        Err(RecvTimeoutError::Timeout) => return Err(...),
        Err(RecvTimeoutError::Disconnected) => return Err(...),
    }
}
\`\`\`

Concerns with the new implementation:

1. \`PollType::Poll\` is non-blocking by contract in wgpu 25. The loop
   relies on \`Poll\` actually driving the \`map_async\` callback forward.
   If a future wgpu point release changes \`Poll\` to be strictly
   \"dispatch-only\" (no callback invocation), the loop would always
   hit the 5s timeout. There is no test pinning this behavior.

2. The \`Disconnected\` branch is reachable only if the \`map_async\`
   closure drops without sending — that happens e.g., if wgpu drops
   the submission without invoking the callback (device lost, internal
   error). No test exercises this path.

3. \`Instant::now() + WEBGPU_READBACK_TIMEOUT\` panics on overflow
   (impossible in practice for 5s, but worth noting as a latent
   concern). Prefer \`checked_add\`.

4. Busy-wait cost: on the timeout path, the loop spins ~500 iterations
   (5000ms / 10ms) calling \`device.poll\` each time. The prior
   \`PollType::Wait\` blocked in the driver. This is a CPU-efficiency
   regression on cancellation/device-loss paths, not a correctness
   regression.

Impact: readback behavior on wgpu failure paths (device lost, long
delay, map_async disconnect) is silently unverified. A correctness
regression in \`PollType::Poll\` semantics between wgpu versions would
silently break screenshots and readback-based features.

Fix direction:
- Add a unit-level test with a mock/stub Device that controls
  \`poll()\` return timing and can force a Disconnected on the mpsc
  channel. Assert each of the four loop outcomes.
- Consider moving the readback loop into a small helper function
  that takes a \`Fn()\` poll closure + channel receiver, to make it
  unit-testable without a real GPU.
- If unit isolation is impractical, at minimum add an integration
  test that triggers a readback and asserts completion within a
  tight CPU budget (regression test for busy-wait blow-up).

Severity: medium — latent risk in a GPU-facing path that typically
works and has no automated coverage for failure modes.

Evidence: commit 6f41130c diff;
crates/frankenterm-gui/src/termwindow/webgpu.rs:168-193 at HEAD.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-20T07:25:33.419495Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-20T08:48:13.062749Z","closed_at":"2026-04-20T08:48:13.062383Z","close_reason":"Added timeout/disconnect/cancel-style readback coverage and hardened the helper deadline with checked_add","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["gpu","review","test-gap","testing","webgpu"]}
-{"id":"ft-b9ndv","title":"[fuzz] add MCP request params parser harness","description":"Existing fuzz coverage already hits pattern-pack TOML/binary, config TOML, workflow YAML, robot envelope JSON, IPC auth envelope, snapshot deserialization, replay recordings, and term/escape parsers. But there is still no fuzz target for MCP request-param parsing.

Evidence:
- `fuzz/Cargo.toml` has no MCP parser target; current targets stop at `ipc_auth_envelope`, `robot_envelope_json`, and related config parsers.
- `crates/frankenterm-core/src/mcp_types.rs:25-170` defines the externally-controlled MCP request param structs (`StateParams`, `GetTextParams`, `SearchParams`, `EventsParams`, etc.), but they are `pub(super)` and only covered by unit/proptest deserialization checks.
- `crates/frankenterm-core/src/mcp_types.rs:1170-1845` has property tests over many `serde_json::from_value(...)` paths, but no byte-level fuzzing against malformed/truncated/oversized JSON.
- `crates/frankenterm-core/src/mcp_framework.rs:203-265` also round-trips untrusted remote MCP payloads through `serde_json::from_value(...)`.

Why it matters:
- MCP is a network/external-input surface with many optional/defaulted fields and multiple tool-specific param shapes.
- Proptests cover valid structured values, not adversarial raw JSON bytes, duplicate keys, deep nesting, type confusion, or truncation.

Fix:
- Add a fuzz seam plus harness that feeds raw JSON/Value payloads through the MCP request-param decode paths (or framework payload bridge) and asserts no panic / deterministic decode outcomes.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","estimated_minutes":45,"created_at":"2026-04-23T19:26:37.331975Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-23T19:26:37.331975Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["fuzz","review"]}

No CRITICAL/HIGH deadlock, starvation, lost-wakeup, or unbounded-buffer defect with a concrete repro was found in runtime.rs during this pass.","status":"closed","priority":4,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-04-23T19:18:02.424603Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-23T19:57:49.325248Z","closed_at":"2026-04-23T19:57:49.324937Z","close_reason":"Audit note recorded; no finding from deadlock-finder pass on runtime.rs","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"ft-bs0ec","title":"robot_types agent inventory golden contract","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"feature","assignee":"pane8","created_at":"2026-04-22T04:22:08.228201Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-22T09:56:43.693909Z","closed_at":"2026-04-22T09:56:43.693614Z","close_reason":"Landed in 97ec6350 (test(frankenterm-core): freeze agent_inventory_contract.json golden for robot payload)","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"ft-btg0h","title":"[fuzz] add frankenterm/term Terminal::advance_bytes target for full escape-to-state pipeline","description":"Surface: frankenterm/term/src/terminal.rs:164 — pub fn advance_bytes<B: AsRef<[u8]>>(&mut self, bytes: B). Master terminal state-machine entry point: raw output bytes → frankenterm_escape_parser → Performer::perform (terminalstate/performer.rs:252) → every CSI/SGR/OSC/DCS dispatch path (perform_csi_mode/window/edit/cursor/sgr in terminalstate/mod.rs:1425-2648, plus image pipelines in terminalstate/sixel.rs, terminalstate/kitty.rs (37KB), terminalstate/iterm.rs). Existing escape_parser_raw fuzz target covers only the lexer stage (bytes → Action); termwiz_csi_parser covers the escape-parser CSI roundtrip property. Nothing exercises the state-machine mutations, cursor/edit bounds, or the Sixel/Kitty/iTerm2 image decoders under arbitrary input.

Proposed harness: fuzz/fuzz_targets/term_advance_bytes.rs — construct a Terminal with a minimal TerminalConfiguration impl, 24x80 size, cap input at 256 KB, feed the whole slice through advance_bytes, drop the terminal. No assertions — libFuzzer asserts no-panic / no-OOM / no-unbounded-alloc.

Corpus seeds: plain text, ESC[H clear, SGR sequences, DECSET/DECRST modes, DECRQM, OSC 52 clipboard, OSC 8 hyperlinks, bracketed paste, CSI u keyboard, DCS sixel fragment (\\x1bPq#0;2;100;0;0#0~~\\x1b\\\\), kitty APC image fragment, iTerm2 OSC 1337 file upload preamble, overlong / truncated / interleaved-with-text variants.

Target must survive 256 KB input without: panic, unbounded allocation (scrollback bounded at 64), unterminated DCS hang, arithmetic overflow in cursor arithmetic, OOB cell index. This discovers sixel/kitty image-decoder arithmetic bugs and any CSI dispatch path that does not clamp its operand to grid bounds.

Add to fuzz/Cargo.toml: frankenterm-term = { path = "../frankenterm/term" }. No new features required — Terminal::advance_bytes is in the crate public API for terminal hosts.

Do NOT duplicate: ft-t4n0z (termwiz InputParser, closed), ft-he0w7 (native_events, closed), ft-p2bjm (replay, in flight), escape_parser_raw (lexer stage only), termwiz_csi_parser (CSI roundtrip property on escape-parser crate, not term::Terminal).","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2026-04-23T07:08:20.022220Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-23T18:29:40.992943Z","closed_at":"2026-04-23T18:29:40.992183Z","close_reason":"Landed in 168d379a (term_advance_bytes harness + corpus); fuzz-build linker blocker documented on bead.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["fuzz","security"],"comments":[{"id":3618,"issue_id":"ft-btg0h","author":"jemanuel","text":"Implemented local diff for term_advance_bytes fuzz harness in fuzz/Cargo.toml, fuzz/fuzz_targets/term_advance_bytes.rs, and fuzz/corpus/term_advance_bytes/*.

Build blocker on verification lane:
1. rch exec -- env CARGO_TARGET_DIR=/tmp/ft-DarkAnchor-target CC=/opt/homebrew/opt/llvm/bin/clang CXX=/opt/homebrew/opt/llvm/bin/clang++ cargo +nightly fuzz build term_advance_bytes
2. rch exec -- env CARGO_TARGET_DIR=/tmp/ft-DarkAnchor-target CC=/opt/homebrew/opt/llvm/bin/clang CXX=/opt/homebrew/opt/llvm/bin/clang++ CARGO_TARGET_AARCH64_APPLE_DARWIN_LINKER=/opt/homebrew/opt/llvm/bin/clang++ cargo +nightly fuzz build term_advance_bytes

Both runs reach the final link for bin term_advance_bytes and fail with the same libfuzzer/c++ symbol error:
Undefined symbols for architecture arm64:
  std::__1::__hash_memory(void const*, unsigned long)
from liblibfuzzer_sys ... FuzzerLoop.o

The second run proves the explicit clang++ linker override does not fix it. Keeping bead open pending toolchain/libfuzzer resolution or alternate verified build lane.","created_at":"2026-04-23T18:20:12Z"}]}

NotificationCooldown::check now bypasses tracking when max_capacity=0, preventing a 1-slot ghost cooldown cache; regression test cooldown_zero_capacity_bypasses_tracking_ft_w80kj added. Verification: rch exec with CC=/opt/homebrew/opt/llvm/bin/clang failed remotely in openssl-sys because the Linux worker cannot resolve that Homebrew clang path; local proof passed via scripts/cargo-local.sh test -p frankenterm-core --lib --no-default-features --features asupersync-runtime cooldown_zero_capacity_bypasses_tracking_ft_w80kj -- --nocapture (1 passed). Default-feature frankenterm-core --lib remains blocked separately by the known web_framework asupersync type-skew beads (ft-h78bk/ft-gfawa/ft-6tr7c).","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"ft-wajba","title":"tmux cmd_queue drop-head-in-flight corrupts Guarded response routing","description":"frankenterm/mux/src/tmux.rs:73-91 (push_command_capped) vs :165-186 (Guarded response handler).

push_command_capped(queue, cmd) does queue.drain(..excess) when queue exceeds CMD_QUEUE_MAX_DEPTH=50_000. The drain is from the FRONT. But when TmuxDomainState.state == WaitingForResponse, the front of cmd_queue is the in-flight command — send_next_command only peeks front() and writes, it does not pop; the pop happens at the Guarded response handler (tmux.rs:172) via cmd_queue.pop_front().

Race / correctness gap: if push_command_capped runs while state == WaitingForResponse and queue.len() reaches 50_001, drain drops the in-flight head. When the Guarded response arrives, pop_front() pops a DIFFERENT pending command and calls cmd.process_result(domain_id, &response) on the wrong command — cross-wiring response payload onto the wrong command parser. Both threads hold the cmd_queue lock so the drain+pop ordering is deterministic under the lock, but the correctness violation stands.

Fix options (not applied here, pattern-level):
 1. When state == WaitingForResponse, skip index 0 in drain: queue.drain(1..=excess) (but preserves head).
 2. Drain from the back (newest-first) which discards most recently enqueued layout/session events that often re-fire.
 3. Abort the in-flight state machine (set state=Idle) and drain — heavy-handed but restores invariant.

Only triggers in pathological event storms (remote tmux lag + 50_000 queued commands) so severity is MEDIUM. The original bug fixed in 1055cffc (push-site cap enforcement) traded unbounded memory for this subtle correctness edge. A regression test would drive tmux into WaitingForResponse, push >50_001 Layout/SessionChanged events, then fire a Guarded response and assert process_result is NOT called against a different command type than the one that was sent.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-21T17:16:32.972876Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-21T17:34:41.760204Z","closed_at":"2026-04-21T17:34:41.759836Z","close_reason":"Shipped in 9cc7e6c2. push_command_capped migrated from free function to TmuxDomainState method so it can consult self.state before choosing drain range:
- state == WaitingForResponse → drain(1..=excess) (preserves index 0, the in-flight cmd awaiting its Guarded response)
- any other state → drain(..excess) (retains existing drop-oldest semantics)

Both drain branches log distinctly so event-storm incidents are traceable. Lock ordering matches Guarded-handler's (cmd_queue first, state transiently inside method): no deadlock. send_next_command's cap-drain is untouched — already guarded by early 'state != Idle → return'.

Regression coverage: 2 new tests under mux::tmux::tests that each seat a distinguishable SplitPane at index 0 and push CMD_QUEUE_MAX_DEPTH more commands; one flips state to WaitingForResponse and asserts the head's get_command() text is unchanged (pre-fix fails), the other keeps state Idle and asserts the head IS drained (pins against future over-correction). Existing cmd_queue_hard_cap_drains_oldest_when_exceeded still green after the refactor.

Verification via rch exec -- env CARGO_TARGET_DIR=/tmp/ft-p5-orch-target:
- cargo check -p mux --lib: clean
- cargo test -p mux --lib cmd_queue: 3/3 green.

Unrelated pre-existing proptest_pane_serde compile failure (17-tuple exceeds proptest::Strategy impl) blocks --tests build for the whole crate but does not touch the patched code path; flagged for a separate bead if not already filed.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"ft-wk5fo","title":"move FTS5 indexing off the capture-write transaction","description":"# Background

Follow-up to ft-kcsae (answered). Live capture path in storage.rs
writes synchronously INTO the FTS5 index via three AFTER-triggers:

    storage.rs:169  output_segments_ai AFTER INSERT  → INSERT INTO output_segments_fts
    storage.rs:173  output_segments_ad AFTER DELETE  → fts delete
    storage.rs:177  output_segments_au AFTER UPDATE  → fts delete + insert

Every captured segment pays the inverted-index rebuild cost before
`append_segment_sync` returns (storage.rs:12308 → 12335 INSERT INTO
output_segments). Under 200-pane fanout the trigger cost is paid 200x
per-wire-tick.

# Existing primitives that can be reused

- `fts_pane_progress` table (storage.rs:472) — already tracks per-pane
  last-indexed-seq with resumable offsets.
- `sync_fts_on_startup(conn, FtsSyncConfig)` (storage.rs:16131) — the
  batched catchup engine. Currently only fires at startup.
- `FtsSyncConfig` (storage.rs:2363) — batch_size=100, max_batch_bytes=1
  MB, commit_progress per batch. Ready knobs.

# Proposal

1. Drop the three AFTER triggers on output_segments.
2. Add a writer-thread ticker (every N seconds OR after M segments
   since last catchup) that runs `sync_fts_on_startup` with the
   existing FtsSyncConfig and uses `fts_pane_progress` to pick up
   where it left off.
3. Gate behind a `storage.fts_indexing_mode = 'sync' | 'deferred'`
   config flag; default 'sync' at first, flip to 'deferred' after a
   round of search-freshness observability.
4. Expose catchup-lag (now - max(fts_pane_progress.last_indexed_at))
   as a metric so operators can spot index drift.

# Risk

- Search freshness becomes eventual (default lag = catchup interval).
- FTS rows can briefly diverge from output_segments during a crash
  between INSERT and the next catchup tick — acceptable because
  `sync_fts_on_startup` already handles gap recovery on startup.

# Measurement prerequisite

Before landing: add a bench that measures `append_segment_sync` p99
under a 200-pane load with FTS5 enabled (current) vs. triggers
disabled + deferred catchup. If the gap is not material, don't ship —
the synchronous path is simpler and the trigger tax might be in the
noise given SQLite's WAL + page cache.","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"pane1","owner":"pane3-fts5-scaffold","created_at":"2026-04-21T18:00:59.588862Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-22T11:28:00.206770Z","closed_at":"2026-04-22T11:28:00.206400Z","close_reason":"Opt-in deferred FTS indexing landed as d74401c6. Added StorageConfig.defer_fts_triggers: bool (default false, preserves current sync behavior). When true, StorageHandle::with_config executes DROP TRIGGER IF EXISTS on output_segments_a[iud] immediately after initialize_schema, so new segment writes no longer synchronously rebuild FTS inverted-index pages. Callers are responsible for periodic sync_fts() invocation; the fts_pane_progress + sync_fts_on_startup engine already handles resumable batched catchup. Verified via 4 new regression tests in fts_sync_tests (17 total passing): fts_sync_mode_indexes_on_insert (baseline), fts_deferred_mode_does_not_index_on_insert (post-DROP empty FTS), fts_deferred_mode_catchup_indexes_all_segments (500 segments → catchup → 500 indexed, second catchup is no-op), fts_deferred_mode_catchup_resumes_across_rounds (5 rounds × 100 segments interleaved with catchup, each round indexes exactly 100). Surfaced but did NOT fix: pre-existing seq=0 off-by-one in sync_fts_for_pane (WHERE seq > last_indexed_seq excludes fresh pane's seq=0 segment) — tests use seq 1..=N, separate bead for that fix.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"comments":[{"id":3602,"issue_id":"ft-wk5fo","author":"pane3-fts5-scaffold","text":"Claimed for scaffolding. -{"id":"ft-wn3yc","title":"[reality-gap] default config export still exposes legacy wa-prefixed paths and prefixes under ft branding","description":"The shipped binary still exports several `wa`-prefixed defaults even though the docs and product surface are branded as `ft`.

 {"id":"ft-wx5ln","title":"pattern pack subdirectories ignore supported rules.yaml/yml/json manifests","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","assignee":"pane8","created_at":"2026-04-22T12:06:44.211855Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-22T12:10:14.601212Z","closed_at":"2026-04-22T12:10:14.600868Z","close_reason":"Landed in 07e72a52: nested pack directories now discover supported rules.yaml/yml/json manifests, with regression coverage.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"ft-wxqbt","title":"mcp_missions::mcp_save_mission_tx_contract_to_path: non-atomic write corrupts tx contract on mid-write crash","description":"crates/frankenterm-core/src/mcp_missions.rs:289-308 mcp_save_mission_tx_contract_to_path serializes a MissionTxContract and calls std::fs::write(path, json) directly. fs::write is NOT atomic — it open+truncate+write in a single sequence. If the process crashes (panic, SIGKILL, power loss, disk full mid-write) between truncate and final write, the target file is left truncated or with partial JSON. On next startup mcp_load_mission_tx_contract_from_path reads the partial file, serde_json::from_str bails with 'Invalid tx contract JSON', and the ENTIRE tx contract is lost — not the last update, the whole in-flight contract. Same shape applies to mcp_save_mission_contract_to_path if it exists via a copy-paste. The codebase already has the atomic-rename pattern for durable writes (config_profiles.rs:129-135 manifest writes, recorder_storage.rs:1061, rulesets.rs:182, wal_engine.rs:892). Fix: write to path.with_extension('json.tmp') first, then std::fs::rename to the final path. rename is atomic on POSIX and NTFS — the tx file is either the old contents or the new contents, never a truncated halfway state.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-22T12:05:53.858137Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-22T12:08:14.359806Z","closed_at":"2026-04-22T12:08:14.359416Z","close_reason":"Landed in 149f35d8 (fix(mcp_missions): atomic tmp+rename for mission/tx-contract saves). Both mcp_save_mission_tx_contract_to_path and mcp_save_mission_to_path now write to <path>.json.tmp and std::fs::rename onto the target. Two regression tests (save_tx_contract_is_atomic_rename_ft_wxqbt, save_mission_is_atomic_rename_ft_wxqbt) assert target file presence + .tmp-sibling absence + round-trip parseability. Verification of the tests blocked by pre-existing frankenterm-core compile blockers (ft-kqidb/uigpj/s2u51/zv3u9).","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
-{"id":"ft-wze7j","title":"[review][LOW] ft-szs5k exhaustive match arm contradicts pre-assert in events_labruntime test","description":"16ed98e5 added a pre-assert AND a silent Cancelled-or-Closed match arm in tests/events_labruntime.rs:198-213. Pre-assert at :200-203 uses matches!(result, Ok or Err Lagged) and panics with 'unexpected recv result' on anything else. Match at :212 accepts Cancelled or Closed silently. If either surfaced, the pre-assert fires FIRST — the match arm is unreachable dead code. Pre-commit state was cleaner: explicit match arm that panicked with 'unexpected close'. Likely caused by compiler warning non-exhaustive after fa1fbffe added Cancelled; the match was patched to add the exhaustive arm just to silence the warning without removing the intent. Pick either: (A) remove pre-assert, restore explicit panic arms, or (B) keep pre-assert, destructure via if-let. Severity: LOW, reviewer round 12, 2026-04-23. Tag: review, tests, cleanup.","status":"open","priority":3,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-23T19:25:37.214441Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-23T19:25:37.214441Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["review"]}

 {"id":"ft-x2fvv","title":"[mor:performance-engineering] PaneTierClassifier takes RwLock.write() on every output byte","description":"# Observation

`PaneTierClassifier::on_pane_output` (pane_tiers.rs:268) acquires
\`panes.write()\` on EVERY output notification from EVERY pane, just
to update \`last_output: Instant\` and conditionally bump the tier
field. The crate has 15 \`.write()\` calls on this single RwLock.

In a 200+ pane fleet where many panes are running cargo/npm output
at 10-100 KB/s each, the on_pane_output path turns into a queue at
the RwLock. Every pane's output fans in through a single exclusive
acquisition.

Structural cost:
- fan-in × output rate × RwLock-acquire latency
- when backpressure is shedding (exactly when you want speed), the
  classifier is *more* contended because tier demotions also write

# Question

Two shapes worth considering:

1. Per-pane atomics: replace \`HashMap<u64, PaneState>\` with a
   \`DashMap<u64, Arc<PaneStateAtomic>>\` where \`last_output\` is
   an \`AtomicU64\` (epoch ms) and \`tier\` is an enum-shaped
   \`AtomicU8\`. on_pane_output becomes a load + CAS, never a
   lock. Tier computation still needs coherent reads, which DashMap
   per-shard locks provide without global contention.

2. Batch ingress: on_pane_output doesn't need to update
   synchronously. Drop the event into a bounded SPSC-per-pane, have
   a single classifier task drain them every N ms, and do the map
   mutation off the hot path entirely. Same consistency model as
   backpressure.

Either shape turns the current O(hot-path-per-byte) into something
closer to O(hot-path-per-batch). Worth a flamegraph under a
200-pane workload before picking.

# Hot-path reasoning

This is the classic "correctness by big lock" pattern. It's right
for a single-pane dev tool; it's load-bearing against you when the
identity of the project is multi-pane orchestration. Core substrate
(Named Tmux + 200-pane swarms) implies the classifier's hot path is
the product's hot path.","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-04-21T17:35:47.955775Z","created_by":"mor-review","updated_at":"2026-04-23T00:18:49.634623Z","closed_at":"2026-04-21T20:35:29.235933Z","close_reason":"Verified in 0b65bc0b","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["hot-path","lock-contention","mor","performance"]}
 {"id":"ft-x4k2b","title":"[reality-gap] runtime_compat 'audited boundary' scope understated — 709 call sites across 83 files","description":"# Claim (README)

README.md:38-42:

    The runtime model is asupersync-native in the product-facing
    APIs: structured, cancel-correct, and centered on Cx-aware
    orchestration across search, policy, workflows, robot mode,
    and the feature-gated web/distributed surfaces. The remaining
    watch-daemon/runtime startup path still crosses a named
    runtime_compat quarantine while the last task-spawn seams are
    being collapsed.

…and:

    `runtime_compat` remains as an audited boundary for runtime
    lifecycle, channel, time, and blocking semantics; it is not
    the product story.

The framing is: small, named, collapsing.

# Reality (code)

    rg -c "runtime_compat::" crates/frankenterm-core/src/
      → 709 call sites across 83 files

The boundary isn't a narrow interop shim — it's the default way
product code spawns tasks, opens channels, sleeps, and times out.
Every capture loop, every workflow step, every web handler, every
mux-pool operation transits it. The "named quarantine" framing
implies 1-2 modules; the code implies every subsystem.

# Why this matters

- **Trust calibration**: operators and contributors use the README
  to calibrate what's "native asupersync" vs. "transitioning." A
  readme that says "the boundary is small" while 83 files sit on
  it gives the wrong picture of where the project is on its own
  milestone (ft-xbnl0).
- **Cancellation semantics**: asupersync's Cx-first path is
  behaviourally different from runtime_compat's tokio fallback
  (see ft-xbnl0 epic for the formal distinction). Callers reading
  the README might assume their task spawns are Cx-aware when
  they're not.
- **Migration progress tracking**: ft-xbnl0.2.x has been landing
  fixes at the task-spawn level for weeks. The README should track
  which specific paths have crossed the threshold and which are
  still on runtime_compat, so operators can calibrate expectations.

# Recommended disposition

Update README.md:38-42 with either a count ("runtime_compat is
still load-bearing for ~700 call sites across 83 files; the
ft-xbnl0 epic tracks collapse"), or a list of the specific
product surfaces still on runtime_compat (watch daemon,
capture loops, workflow steps, web handlers).

Either form gives operators a calibrated picture without
overstating or understating the state.

# Related

- ft-2guux G1 raised the same concern about the watch daemon
  specifically; closed with a README clarification.
- ft-xbnl0 is the open epic tracking the full migration. This bead
  is specifically about the README framing, not the migration itself.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-04-21T20:45:20.507726Z","created_by":"reality-check","updated_at":"2026-04-23T00:18:56.093751Z","closed_at":"2026-04-21T20:50:22.624765Z","close_reason":"done","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["docs","reality-gap","runtime"]}
 {"id":"ft-x50ce","title":"[MEDIUM] conformal::ConformalPredictor silently narrows prediction intervals when coverage=NaN","description":"# Defect

`crates/frankenterm-core/src/conformal.rs:286` — `ConformalPredictor::new` stores `coverage: coverage.clamp(0.01, 0.999)`.

Same `f64::clamp(NaN, _, _) = NaN` gotcha as ft-761tz / ft-b4l62 / ft-jz40p / ft-0p5q5. When `coverage = NaN`, the stored value stays NaN. On every `forecast_all` call:

1. `cal.quantile(self.coverage)` (line 326) is called with NaN coverage.
2. `CalibrationBuffer::quantile` (line 213) computes `k = ((coverage * (n+1)).ceil() as usize).max(1)` → `NaN.ceil() = NaN` → `NaN as usize = 0` → `k = 1`.
3. `k > n` is false (n ≥ 1), so falls through.
4. Returns `sorted[0]` — the **smallest** calibration score.
5. `forecast_all` wraps `(point - q, point + q)` using the smallest q possible — producing the **narrowest** prediction interval the calibration data can support.

**Net**: operators set `coverage = NaN` (via config typo, arithmetic bug upstream, deserialization of an invalid input), and the predictor silently undercuts the formal-coverage guarantee by emitting the tightest possible intervals instead of the widest. Intervals labelled `coverage: NaN` in the output look broken but are still shipped; downstream alerting thresholds driven by these intervals over-fire.

## Severity rationale

- Silent statistical wrong-answer: the predictor's entire point is the formal coverage guarantee; NaN undermines it silently.
- Not a crash or data loss.
- Input reachable via config deserialization or external arithmetic (e.g., `1.0 - alpha` where alpha came from a NaN upstream).

## Fix

Guard at the new() boundary: if `coverage.is_nan()`, snap to the default 0.95 (same as `ConformalConfig::default()` at line 76). Alternative would be to reject and return Result, but callers signature is fn new() -> Self, so keep the infallible default. A tracing::warn log helps debug.

Also tighten `CalibrationBuffer::quantile` at line 213 so even if `self.coverage` is ever NaN (future code paths, direct callers), the function returns None instead of silently-smallest. Same pattern as ft-b4l62/ft-jz40p.

## Tests

* `ft_x50ce_predictor_rejects_nan_coverage_at_construction` — `new(..., coverage: NaN, ..)` stores 0.95 default, not NaN.
* `ft_x50ce_quantile_nan_coverage_returns_none` — `CalibrationBuffer::quantile(NaN)` returns None (defense in depth).

## Scope

Single file (`conformal.rs`).","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-22T17:29:12.968247Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-22T17:33:02.964336Z","closed_at":"2026-04-22T17:33:02.963917Z","close_reason":"done","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
@@ -370,7 +370,7 @@
 {"id":"ft-xgs7n","title":"[testing-fuzzing] pin extract_delta overlap-existence invariant: previous.ends_with(current[..len-delta.len()])","description":"# Gap

proptest_ingest_extract_delta_fuzz.rs covers 5 MRs (crash-freedom,
identity, content-suffix, pure-append, determinism) + 2 inline
regression tests, but does NOT pin the primary semantic contract of
the algorithm: **when Content(delta) is returned, the non-delta head
of `current` must be a tail of `previous`**. Formally:

    extract_delta(previous, current, _) = Content(delta)
    ⇒ previous.ends_with(&current[..current.len() - delta.len()])

This is the invariant the sliding-window search ACTUALLY proves
(search_window[pos..] == current[..overlap_len], where
search_window = previous[search_start..], so previous ends with
current[..overlap_len]). Without it, the algorithm could return a
Content(delta) where the "non-delta prefix" is pure fabrication —
the existing MR3 suffix-check proves current ends with delta but
says nothing about the overlap being real.

Adding this test catches any regression in the overlap-candidate
acceptance logic (`search_window[pos..] == current[..overlap_len]`
on ingest.rs:1711) that accidentally accepts a non-matching window.

Fix: new proptest in proptest_ingest_extract_delta_fuzz.rs covering
every path (pure-append fast path + sliding-window search).","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2026-04-22T10:04:40.819353Z","created_by":"pane3-ingest-mr","updated_at":"2026-04-22T10:13:32.759851Z","closed_at":"2026-04-22T10:13:32.759521Z","close_reason":"Landed in 5eb9f956 (fix(apc, backpressure, ingest): …, overlap-existence proptest) — extract_delta_content_overlap_exists_in_previous added to tests/proptest_ingest_extract_delta_fuzz.rs:207. Verified: 1 passed / 0 failed / 14 filtered out on --test proptest_ingest_extract_delta_fuzz (test run took 0.20s under 512 proptest cases). Pins the semantic contract that when Content(delta) returns, previous.ends_with(current[..current.len() - delta.len()]) — the 'overlap was real' claim that MR3's suffix-only check does not catch.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["ingest","proptest","testing"]}
 {"id":"ft-xicum","title":"session_restore delete_session returns false after cleaning orphaned checkpoint rows","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","assignee":"pane8","created_at":"2026-04-22T11:50:56.388120Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-22T11:52:53.130618Z","closed_at":"2026-04-22T11:52:53.130299Z","close_reason":"delete_session now returns true when it removed orphaned checkpoint or pane-state rows, not just when the parent mux_sessions row existed; added regression coverage for orphan cleanup.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"ft-xkuw9","title":"retry: ft-gc4hz regression test references private telemetry fields (build broken)","description":"Commit 765743d5 shipped cancelled_error_does_not_trip_circuit_breaker_ft_gc4hz which reads circuit.telemetry().failures_recorded and .successes_recorded directly. These fields are private on CircuitBreakerTelemetry — only the snapshot() accessor is public. Result: frankenterm-core does not compile under --no-default-features (E0616 at retry.rs:880:44 and :881:44). Fix landed in this commit: switch to circuit.telemetry().snapshot() and read the Snapshot's public fields.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-22T10:15:31.502945Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-22T10:22:49.654446Z","closed_at":"2026-04-22T10:22:49.653600Z","close_reason":"Landed in 79029eeb — telemetry().snapshot() replaces private-field access in the ft-gc4hz regression test. crate now compiles on retry.rs's behalf; ft-kqidb still blocks full build.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
-{"id":"ft-xo3u4","title":"[review][MEDIUM] ft-djpi3 wa.events_annotate changed-bit derivation is TOCTOU-racy across concurrent annotate calls","description":"commit 0a865e6f derives wa.events_annotate's 'changed' field by READ-WRITE-READ-DIFF across four independent storage calls with NO surrounding transaction at crates/frankenterm-core/src/mcp_tools.rs:4292-4368:

    let prior_annotations = storage.get_event_annotations(params.event_id).await?...;   // TXN 1 (read)
    let prior_note = prior_annotations.note.clone();
    storage.set_event_note(params.event_id, params.note.clone(), params.by.clone()).await?;   // TXN 2 (write)
    ... record audit ...                                                                 // TXN 3 (write)
    let annotations = storage.get_event_annotations(params.event_id).await?...;          // TXN 4 (read)
    let changed = prior_note != annotations.note;

Two concurrent wa.events_annotate calls (same event_id, different notes) race:

    Client A: TXN 1 reads prior=None
    Client B: TXN 1 reads prior=None    <-- same pre-state
    Client A: TXN 2 writes 'foo'
    Client B: TXN 2 writes 'bar'        <-- B overwrites A
    Client A: TXN 4 reads note='bar'    <-- A sees B's write, NOT its own
    Client A: changed = None != Some('bar') = TRUE   <-- A claims success despite being overwritten
    Client B: TXN 4 reads note='bar'
    Client B: changed = None != Some('bar') = TRUE

Client A's envelope reports changed=true AND annotations.note='bar' — but A wrote 'foo'. An agent reading the envelope would conclude 'my annotation is live' when it isn't. For multi-agent swarms that use wa.events_annotate to leave a handoff trace on events, this is silent data loss.

The first-event-no-prior case is handled correctly (changed=true for a first write that transitions None→Some; changed=false for clear-on-already-empty) — tests events_annotate_reports_changed_for_real_write_and_noop_rewrite and events_annotate_reports_changed_false_when_clearing_absent_note at :5276-5336 cover the serial case.

## Fix direction

Option A (smallest diff): wrap the read-write-read sequence in a single SQLite transaction so the prior/new reads are consistent with this call's write.

Option B: have set_event_note return (prior_note, new_note) atomically from within its own UPDATE statement (e.g., RETURNING clause + snapshot); compute changed from the return value, no second read needed.

Option C: make the 'changed' semantics 'the write landed and differed from THIS CALL's observation of prior state' — document that concurrent callers may both see changed=true. Weaker contract but simpler.

## Why MEDIUM, not HIGH

- wa.events_annotate concurrency pressure in current deployments is low (annotation is an operator action, not a high-rate path).
- Data loss is bounded (last-write-wins, standard SQLite semantics); the misleading 'changed' bit is reporting-level only.
- Not a ship-blocker; a Linear-FIXME-style follow-up.

Severity: MEDIUM. Audit round 15, 2026-04-23. Tag: review, mcp, concurrency, toctou.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-23T19:50:13.946117Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-23T19:50:13.946117Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["review"]}
+{"id":"ft-xo3u4","title":"[review][MEDIUM] ft-djpi3 wa.events_annotate changed-bit derivation is TOCTOU-racy across concurrent annotate calls","description":"commit 0a865e6f derives wa.events_annotate's 'changed' field by READ-WRITE-READ-DIFF across four independent storage calls with NO surrounding transaction at crates/frankenterm-core/src/mcp_tools.rs:4292-4368:

    let prior_annotations = storage.get_event_annotations(params.event_id).await?...;   // TXN 1 (read)
    let prior_note = prior_annotations.note.clone();
    storage.set_event_note(params.event_id, params.note.clone(), params.by.clone()).await?;   // TXN 2 (write)
    ... record audit ...                                                                 // TXN 3 (write)
    let annotations = storage.get_event_annotations(params.event_id).await?...;          // TXN 4 (read)
    let changed = prior_note != annotations.note;

Two concurrent wa.events_annotate calls (same event_id, different notes) race:

    Client A: TXN 1 reads prior=None
    Client B: TXN 1 reads prior=None    <-- same pre-state
    Client A: TXN 2 writes 'foo'
    Client B: TXN 2 writes 'bar'        <-- B overwrites A
    Client A: TXN 4 reads note='bar'    <-- A sees B's write, NOT its own
    Client A: changed = None != Some('bar') = TRUE   <-- A claims success despite being overwritten
    Client B: TXN 4 reads note='bar'
    Client B: changed = None != Some('bar') = TRUE

Client A's envelope reports changed=true AND annotations.note='bar' — but A wrote 'foo'. An agent reading the envelope would conclude 'my annotation is live' when it isn't. For multi-agent swarms that use wa.events_annotate to leave a handoff trace on events, this is silent data loss.

The first-event-no-prior case is handled correctly (changed=true for a first write that transitions None→Some; changed=false for clear-on-already-empty) — tests events_annotate_reports_changed_for_real_write_and_noop_rewrite and events_annotate_reports_changed_false_when_clearing_absent_note at :5276-5336 cover the serial case.

## Fix direction

Option A (smallest diff): wrap the read-write-read sequence in a single SQLite transaction so the prior/new reads are consistent with this call's write.

Option B: have set_event_note return (prior_note, new_note) atomically from within its own UPDATE statement (e.g., RETURNING clause + snapshot); compute changed from the return value, no second read needed.

Option C: make the 'changed' semantics 'the write landed and differed from THIS CALL's observation of prior state' — document that concurrent callers may both see changed=true. Weaker contract but simpler.

## Why MEDIUM, not HIGH

- wa.events_annotate concurrency pressure in current deployments is low (annotation is an operator action, not a high-rate path).
- Data loss is bounded (last-write-wins, standard SQLite semantics); the misleading 'changed' bit is reporting-level only.
- Not a ship-blocker; a Linear-FIXME-style follow-up.

Severity: MEDIUM. Audit round 15, 2026-04-23.  {"id":"ft-xv561","title":"[HIGH] pattern-engine regex construction has no backtrack limit — workspace-local ReDoS via user packs","description":"Found during /security-audit-for-saas pass #2 (pane 5, 2026-04-21).

## Summary
The pattern engine compiles user-supplied regex patterns via
`fancy_regex::Regex::new(raw)` with NO backtrack limit or match
timeout. An attacker who can drop a YAML/JSON pattern pack into
`~/.config/wa/patterns/` or a repo's `.ft/patterns/` directory can
ship a catastrophic-backtracking regex that hangs `ft watch` the
first time matching pane output triggers the pattern's anchor.

## Evidence
- crates/frankenterm-core/src/patterns.rs:13
  `use fancy_regex::Regex;`
- patterns.rs:512 (RuleDef::validate_regex)
  `Regex::new(regex).map_err(...)` — no `.backtrack_limit(...)`.
- patterns.rs:780 (build_engine_index)
  `let regex = Regex::new(raw).map_err(...)` — same.
- patterns.rs:920 (load_packs_from_config) + :935
  (load_pack_from_id) — pack files are loaded from
  `config.resolved_user_packs_dir()` (default
  `~/.config/wa/patterns/`) AND from `.ft/patterns/` inside the
  resolved workspace root. The latter is an attacker-controlled
  directory when a repo is cloned from an untrusted source.
- mcp.rs:2010 (pattern-engine matching hot path)
  invokes `regex.captures_iter(text)` on every segment for every
  rule whose anchor matched. A single-threaded ReDoS blocks the
  matching worker; fancy_regex's default backtracking engine has
  no internal budget.

## Attack vector
1. attacker commits `.ft/patterns/evil.json` with a rule like
   ```json
   {
     "id": "evil.dos",
     "agent_type": "wezterm",
     "event_type": "noop",
     "anchors": ["a"],
     "regex": "^(a+)+$"
   }
   ```
2. user clones repo and runs `ft watch`.
3. the first pane output containing `"aaa...aaab"` (any near-match
   of the anchor) triggers captures_iter, which enters catastrophic
   backtracking. The pattern worker hangs indefinitely, wedging the
   detection pipeline.

No code execution — DoS only. But `ft watch` is the primary
operator surface; wedging the detection loop silently destroys
the "intelligent detection" claim the README makes.

## Fix direction
Two layers:

1. Compilation-time gate (quick):
   - Replace `Regex::new(raw)` with `fancy_regex::RegexBuilder::new(raw).backtrack_limit(1_000_000).build()`
     at patterns.rs:512 and :780. 1M steps is plenty for any
     legitimate rule and caps worst-case match cost.
   - Propagate the resulting `fancy_regex::Error::RuntimeError` from
     `captures_iter` as a `Detection` of kind `RegexRuntimeError`
     instead of hanging.

2. Match-time budget (robust):
   - Wrap each `regex.captures_iter(text)` call in a time-bounded
     harness (e.g., spawn a short-lived asupersync task with a
     100ms timeout). On timeout, quarantine the offending rule and
     surface a "rule disabled due to catastrophic match cost"
     telemetry event so operators can triage.

## Severity rationale
- HIGH (P1): attacker-controlled repo → DoS on user's machine.
- Not CRITICAL: local exploitation only (repo-clone vector), no
  network reach, no data exfil, no code execution.

## Out of scope for this bead
- RE2/Hyperscan migration (much larger rework).
- Per-rule memory budget (separate concern).

## Related
- ft-security-mcp-path-traversal (30988e53) — same audit pass
  already fixed the MCP arbitrary-file-read vector.

Filed per /security-audit-for-saas pass #2; recommend shipping the
compilation-time gate within one follow-up session.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-21T04:04:22.269417Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-21T04:14:08.976852Z","closed_at":"2026-04-21T04:14:08.976108Z","close_reason":"Fixed in 070117a0: fix(patterns): cap regex backtracking at 10M steps to close ft-xv561 pattern-engine ReDoS on attacker-controlled pattern packs","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["audit","patterns","redos","security"]}
